text.title: ML/AI Decision Framework Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules that every decision record must satisfy. Each is enforced by the validator and cited by the decision tree.

rule.statement: Decision starts with prompt engineering ($0 extra, hours to deploy). RAG is only added when external/private/changing data is required. Fine-tuning is only added when behavioral specialization at scale is required (≥1M requests/month). Each step up the stack must justify its added cost+complexity in the decision record.
rule.rationale: Skipping the prompt step and going straight to fine-tuning is the most common over-engineering pattern; it adds 6× inference cost + dataset maintenance + retraining cadence for quality gains that prompting often matches.

rule.statement: RAG is the chosen approach IFF at least one of: (a) corpus changes daily/weekly, (b) data is private/proprietary, (c) source citations are a hard product requirement, (d) corpus exceeds the chosen model's context window after compression.

rationale.source: OpenAI Retrieval guide; Anthropic RAG patterns 2026

rule.rationale: RAG carries retrieval latency, chunking artefacts, and index maintenance overhead — for <50 stable documents the right answer is to stuff them into the prompt with caching, not stand up a vector DB.

rule.statement: Fine-tuning is the chosen approach IFF all hold: (a) the specialization need is behavioral (style, persona, jargon, format) NOT factual recall, (b) data is stable (updates ≤monthly), (c) volume is ≥1M requests/month OR the latency/quality SLO cannot be hit any other way. Decision record MUST include the ROI calculation: (training_cost + 6× inference_premium + maintenance) vs (quality_gain × volume × value_per_quality_unit).

rationale.source: OpenAI Fine-tuning docs 2026; HF PEFT cost analyses

rule.rationale: Fine-tuning bakes patterns into weights — irreversible short-term, expensive to maintain. Below the volume threshold prompt engineering + RAG almost always beats FT on cost-quality.

rule.statement: Model tier is chosen by task complexity, not by default. Simple extraction/classification → Haiku-tier / DeepSeek V3 / GPT-mini. Standard generation → Sonnet / GPT-4o / Gemini 2 Pro. Irreversible-error tasks (medical, legal, refunds, deletions) OR deep multi-step reasoning → Opus / GPT-frontier.

rationale.source: Anthropic Pricing 2026; OpenAI Pricing 2026; Gemini pricing

rule.rationale: Frontier-by-default overspends 2-3× on traffic that Haiku-class would have handled identically.

rule.statement: Every decision record carries quarterly_review_due (date = decision_date + 90 days). On that date the decision is re-validated against current model pricing, deprecation notices, and any new providers. If unchanged, advance by 90 days; if changed, open a new record.

rationale.source: finops.org cadence; OpenAI / Anthropic deprecation history 2024-2026

rule.rationale: Model pricing has shifted ≥30% per quarter for multiple providers; deprecations remove options mid-roadmap; teams locked into year-old decisions silently overspend.

rule.statement: Fine-tuning is forbidden when the goal is factual recall. Factual specialization MUST be solved by RAG. Decision record MUST classify the specialization need as behavioral OR factual before choosing the approach.
rule.rationale: Fine-tuning on facts entrenches a snapshot in weights — facts change, weights don't. RAG keeps facts current; FT keeps style consistent.
